coreJobStatement: Deliver structured K-12 academic instruction to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and supervised facility environment
productJobStatement: Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use during occupied school hours while ensuring instant single-motion evacuation capability per IBC / NFPA 101 and generating auditable compliance documentation
lPath: L5.d.2 — Emergency Exit Alarm & Monitoring Systems → L6d — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management

segments.name: Aging-stock district
segments.characteristics: Average building age 49 years; no conduit to secondary exits; wiring retrofit cost-prohibitive
segments.targetabilityReason: Wireless-only path; strong ROI on avoiding electrical work
segments.circumstance: Average building age 49 years; no conduit to secondary exits; wiring retrofit cost-prohibitive
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — wireless-only path; strong ROI on avoiding electrical work

segments.name: Post-Uvalde compliance mandate
segments.characteristics: School in 32+ US states with new door-security legislation; procurement driven by law not discretion
segments.targetabilityReason: Safety budget unlocked; compliance deadline creates urgency
segments.circumstance: School in 32+ US states with new door-security legislation; procurement driven by law not discretion
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — safety budget unlocked; compliance deadline creates urgency

segments.name: Multi-campus district portfolio
segments.characteristics: 10+ schools under single facilities director; centralized monitoring is the value driver, not per-door cost
segments.targetabilityReason: Dashboard/aggregation value dominates; per-unit economics secondary
segments.circumstance: 10+ schools under single facilities director; centralized monitoring is the value driver, not per-door cost
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — dashboard/aggregation value dominates; per-unit economics secondary

segments.name: High-traffic perimeter zone
segments.characteristics: Schools with gyms, cafeterias, or sports fields accessed via secondary exits multiple times daily
segments.targetabilityReason: Battery life constraint (C4) dominant; false alarm management critical
segments.circumstance: Schools with gyms, cafeterias, or sports fields accessed via secondary exits multiple times daily
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — battery life constraint (C4) dominant; false alarm management critical

segments.name: Private / charter school
segments.characteristics: Independent procurement authority; can act without LEA approval cycle; often tighter budgets
segments.targetabilityReason: Faster sales cycle; price sensitivity higher; IoT ROI must be self-evident
segments.circumstance: Independent procurement authority; can act without LEA approval cycle; often tighter budgets
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — faster sales cycle; price sensitivity higher; IoT ROI must be self-evident

alternatives.name: Wired panic hardware + alarm panel integration
alternatives.tradeoffs: High installation labor; conduit to each door; retrofit disruption in 49-yr-old buildings; ongoing panel maintenance; certified integrator required. ~55% market share.

alternatives.name: Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Zero monitoring: staff learn of events only by physical discovery or triggered alarm heard locally; no compliance log; no remote visibility. ~30% market share.

alternatives.name: Wireless IoT alarmed exit guards (Tuerwachter category)
alternatives.tradeoffs: Battery management overhead; wireless range limited in concrete (C3/C4); proprietary protocol (C6); no native integration with BACnet/KNX. ~15% market share.

alternatives.name: Security personnel stationed / patrolling exits
alternatives.tradeoffs: High recurring labor cost; coverage gaps during breaks/shifts; human error; no automated log; scales poorly to multi-building campuses. <5% market share.

alternatives.name: Do nothing / accept risk
alternatives.tradeoffs: Zero P1 burden; compliance violation risk; insurance liability; state audit findings; zero monitoring of unauthorized entry through fire exits. Most common current state in budget-constrained districts.

jobSteps.description: Determine which exit doors require monitoring, what alarm parameters apply, and what coverage zone each receiver must serve
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Coverage gaps in the scoping decision propagate to unmonitored doors
jobSteps.rawStatement: Determine which exit doors require monitoring, what alarm parameters apply, and what coverage zone each receiver must serve
jobSteps.rationale: Every deployment starts with a scoping decision; gaps here cause missed coverage

jobSteps.description: Locate available infrastructure paths, power sources, and network access points for receiver and gateway placement
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Wireless IoT minimizes this step; receiver placement still requires assessment but is not the primary pain point
jobSteps.rawStatement: Locate available power sources, network drops, and structural paths for any infrastructure components
jobSteps.rationale: Wireless IoT minimizes this step, but receiver placement still requires assessment

jobSteps.description: Install door alarm units and supporting infrastructure (receivers, gateways, repeaters); configure system for monitoring operation
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Installation burden is the primary procurement barrier for aging buildings
jobSteps.rawStatement: Install door alarm units and infrastructure (receivers, gateways); configure system for monitoring
jobSteps.rationale: Installation burden is the primary procurement barrier for aging buildings

jobSteps.description: Verify all door alarm units are functional and monitoring is active before occupied school hours begin
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Daily pre-check; undetected device faults accumulate here
jobSteps.rawStatement: Verify all door units are functional before occupied school hours begin
jobSteps.rationale: Daily pre-check; failure-to-confirm is where undetected device faults accumulate

jobSteps.description: Monitor all designated exit doors in real time during occupied school hours; generate staff alerts on activation events
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Core safety function; primary value delivered here
jobSteps.rawStatement: Monitor all exit doors in real time during occupied school hours; generate alerts on activation events
jobSteps.rationale: Core function; primary safety value delivered here

jobSteps.description: Track device health (battery level, signal strength) and detect coverage drift before it causes monitoring failures
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Battery depletion is the dominant reliability failure mode for wireless IoT
jobSteps.rawStatement: Track device health (battery, signal strength, firmware); detect coverage drift
jobSteps.rationale: Battery depletion is the dominant reliability failure mode for wireless IoT

jobSteps.description: Respond to alarm events; silence confirmed alarms; re-arm doors; update device configuration as needed
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: False alarm management and re-arm speed determine operational acceptance
jobSteps.rawStatement: Respond to alarm events; silence confirmed alarms; re-arm doors; update configuration
jobSteps.rationale: False alarm management and re-arm speed determine operational acceptance

jobSteps.description: Log all door events; generate compliance documentation for fire marshal inspections and state school safety audits
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Post-Uvalde compliance documentation is a named purchasing driver in 32+ US states
jobSteps.rawStatement: Log all door events; generate compliance reports; prepare audit evidence for fire marshal / state safety inspection
jobSteps.rationale: Post-Uvalde compliance documentation is a named purchasing driver

stakeholders.pyramidLevels: P1 ALL, P2, P3, P4, P5

stakeholders.who: State safety auditor, fire marshal, insurance broker

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of critical emergency exit points being omitted from the monitoring plan due to incomplete site assessment
Minimize the total electrician labor hours required to install exit alarm monitoring systems at secondary and perimeter doors
Minimize the likelihood that installation of exit alarm units requires structural modification to existing door frame or door hardware
Minimize the number of additional receiver and repeater infrastructure units required to achieve full wireless coverage across all exit points in a multi-story school building
Minimize the time required to verify that all exit alarm units are operational at the start of each school day
Minimize the likelihood of device failures that remain undetected until an alarm event occurs during occupied school hours
Minimize the integration complexity required to connect exit door monitoring events to the district's existing access control or building management platform
Minimize the time elapsed between an unauthorized exit door activation and first staff notification during occupied school hours
Minimize the frequency of false alarm events triggered by thermal door expansion, wind pressure, or incidental handle contact
Increase the ability to distinguish between a propped-open event, an alarm-triggered exit, and an authorized bypass activation on the monitoring dashboard
Minimize the degradation of alarm transmission coverage in building areas with reinforced concrete construction or metallic structural elements
Minimize the likelihood of missed alarm transmissions caused by radio frequency interference from co-located wireless networks in the school building
Minimize the number of monitoring coverage gaps caused by undetected battery depletion before door alarm operation is affected
Increase the ability to receive advance warning of battery status degradation at individual door units before monitoring capability is lost
Minimize the time required to identify which specific door unit is experiencing a monitoring failure across a multi-building campus
Minimize the effort required to re-arm a door alarm unit after an authorized exit event without specialized tools or access credentials
Minimize the likelihood of an alarm-silencing action occurring without generating a corresponding audit log entry for compliance records
Minimize the effort required to register a replacement door alarm unit with the existing receiver network without installer software access
Minimize the labor time required to compile door event logs into a format acceptable for state fire safety and school security audits
Minimize the likelihood of compliance audit findings related to gaps in exit monitoring documentation during annual fire marshal inspections
Increase the ability to present real-time exit monitoring compliance status to district administrators, school board members, or state inspectors without manual record assembly
Increase the ability to present auditable exit compliance records demonstrating that post-Uvalde legislative requirements were continuously met without incident
Minimize the likelihood that an unsecured emergency exit is visible to media, parents, or state officials before campus staff have received notification
Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether all emergency exits in the building are currently secured during occupied school hours
